Name: cross_modal_spectral_alignment
Title: Cross-Modal Spectral Alignment for Training-Free Latency-Aware Multimodal Inference
Short Hypothesis: Aligning the spectral properties of activations across modalities can enable training-free, latency-aware inference optimization in multimodal models without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work has explored spectral-based inference optimization, but primarily within single modalities (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing). Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation leverages geometric properties but focuses on language models. Our proposal extends these ideas to multimodal settings, using cross-modal spectral alignment as a unified framework for inference optimization.
Abstract: Multimodal models have shown impressive capabilities but often suffer from inefficient inference due to the heterogeneous nature of different modalities. We propose Cross-Modal Spectral Alignment (CMSA), a training-free framework that optimizes inference efficiency by aligning the spectral properties of activations across modalities. CMSA computes the dominant spectral directions for each modality using randomized SVD and identifies a shared low-rank subspace that captures the essential information for the task. During inference, activations are projected onto this subspace, reducing computational overhead while preserving accuracy. We provide theoretical guarantees linking the alignment of spectral properties to inference stability and generalization performance. Experiments on multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that CMSA achieves superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to existing single-modality optimization methods, offering a unified approach for efficient multimodal inference.
Experiments: Compute dominant spectral directions for each modality using randomized SVD on a small calibration set.
Identify a shared low-rank subspace by aligning the spectral directions across modalities.
Project activations onto the shared subspace during inference, reducing the dimensionality of computations.
Evaluate on multimodal benchmarks (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo) under varying latency constraints.
Compare throughput-accuracy trade-offs against single-modality optimization baselines (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing).
Measure spectral alignment stability and its impact on inference efficiency and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of spectral alignment may vary across different multimodal architectures and tasks.
Identifying a shared subspace could introduce additional computational overhead during the calibration phase.
The approach assumes that essential information for the task is captured in the low-rank subspace, which may not hold for all tasks.
Theoretical guarantees rely on assumptions about the spectral properties of activations, which may not always be met in practice.
